title: The Boot Chain: Firmware to Init

lessons.title: The Big Picture: From Power Button to Login
lessons.content:
Have you ever wondered what happens in the few seconds between pressing the power button and seeing a login screen? The computer doesn't wake up all at once. It comes alive in **stages**, and each stage's only job is to start the next one. This sequence is called the **boot chain** ("to boot" comes from "bootstrap" — the machine pulling itself up by its own bootstraps).

Think of a relay race. Each runner carries the baton a short distance, then hands it to the next runner. No single runner does the whole race. The boot chain works the same way:

1. **Firmware** — a small program built into the computer's motherboard. It checks the hardware is working, then finds a disk to start from.
2. **Bootloader** — a program on that disk (the common one is called GRUB). Its job is to load the operating system's core, the kernel.
3. **Kernel** — the heart of Linux. It takes control of the hardware and gets the system ready.
4. **init** — the very first regular program. Today this is almost always a program called **systemd**. It starts all the other services (networking, sound, the desktop) until you reach a login prompt.

Why learn this? Because when a computer won't boot, the screen usually freezes at *one* of these stages. Knowing the chain tells you which runner dropped the baton — and that is the first step to fixing it.

**Recap:** power on → firmware → bootloader → kernel → init → login. Five handoffs, in that order. Everything else in this sub-topic just zooms in on one stage at a time.

items.prompt: Put the boot stages in the correct order, from pressing the power button to reaching a login prompt.
choices: Power on → kernel → firmware → bootloader → init → login
Power on → firmware → bootloader → kernel → init → login
Power on → bootloader → firmware → init → kernel → login
Power on → init → kernel → bootloader → firmware → login
items.explanation: The order is firmware first (it is built into the hardware and runs before anything on disk), then the bootloader it finds on disk, which loads the kernel, which finally starts init. Remembering this order is the single most useful thing in this sub-topic.

items.prompt: What is the main idea behind the term "boot chain"?
choices: The computer does everything at once the moment you press power
The computer wakes up in stages, and each stage's job is to start the next one
The chain refers to physically chaining several disks together
It is the cable that connects the power button to the motherboard
items.explanation: Booting happens in stages that hand off one to the next, like a relay race passing a baton. Nothing happens "all at once," and the word has nothing to do with cables or chaining disks.

items.prompt: Which stage runs first, before anything stored on the hard disk gets a chance to run?
choices: The kernel
The bootloader
The firmware
init (systemd)
items.explanation: Firmware is built into the motherboard itself, so it is the only thing available the instant power arrives. The bootloader, kernel, and init all live on a disk and cannot run until the firmware finds that disk.

items.prompt: What is the bootloader's main job in the chain?
choices: To test that the memory and hardware are working
To load the operating system's kernel into memory and start it
To show you the login prompt
To start the networking and sound services
items.explanation: The bootloader (commonly GRUB) sits between the firmware and the kernel. Hardware testing is the firmware's job; the login prompt and starting services come later, after init takes over.

items.prompt: On a typical modern Linux system, which program is started as the very first regular program (init)?
choices: GRUB
systemd
The firmware
The login screen
items.explanation: Today init is almost always systemd. GRUB is the bootloader (an earlier stage), the firmware runs even earlier, and the login screen only appears after init has started the needed services.

items.prompt: Why is understanding the boot chain useful when a computer refuses to start?
choices: It lets you skip the boot process entirely
The screen usually freezes at one particular stage, and knowing the stages tells you where the failure is
It makes the computer boot faster automatically
It is only useful for designing new computers, not fixing them
items.explanation: A boot failure happens at a specific stage. If you know the chain, the point where it freezes tells you which "runner dropped the baton," which is the first step toward a fix. It does not speed anything up by itself.

items.prompt: After the kernel finishes its early setup, what does init (systemd) do?
choices: It tests the computer's hardware
It loads the kernel from disk
It starts the other services until you reach a login prompt
It shuts the computer back down
items.explanation: init is the parent of all other programs; it starts services such as networking and the desktop, ending at a login prompt. Testing hardware is the firmware's job, and loading the kernel is the bootloader's.

items.prompt: The relay-race analogy describes the boot chain as a series of handoffs. What is being "handed off" at each step?
choices: A physical baton inside the computer
Control of the machine, passed from one stage to the next
Electrical power, which each stage uses up
The user's password
items.explanation: Each stage does a small job and then hands control to the next stage. There is no real baton; it is a way to picture how responsibility passes along the chain. Power and passwords are not what gets passed.

items.prompt: Which statement about the kernel is correct, based on the big picture?
choices: The kernel runs before the firmware
The kernel is loaded by the bootloader and takes control of the hardware
The kernel is the same thing as the login screen
The kernel is built into the motherboard
items.explanation: The bootloader loads the kernel, which then runs the hardware as the heart of Linux. The firmware runs earlier, the kernel is not the login screen, and only the firmware is built into the motherboard.

items.prompt: Look at the diagram of the boot chain. Reading it left to right, which sequence does it show?
choices: Login → init → kernel → bootloader → firmware → power
Power button → firmware → bootloader → kernel+initramfs → init (systemd) → login
Firmware → power button → kernel → login → init
Power button → kernel → firmware → init → bootloader → login
items.explanation: The diagram reads left to right as a relay of handoffs: power, then firmware, then the bootloader, then the kernel (with its initramfs), then init/systemd, ending at the login prompt — the same order to memorize.

lessons.title: Firmware: UEFI and Old BIOS
lessons.content:
When you press power, the computer needs *some* program to run — but nothing useful is in memory yet, because the operating system lives on a disk that hasn't been read. The fix: a small program is permanently stored in a chip on the motherboard. That program is the **firmware**. "Firmware" just means software baked into hardware so it's always there.

The firmware has two jobs. First, a quick hardware check often called **POST** (Power-On Self-Test): is there memory, a keyboard, a disk? Second, **find a boot device** — a disk that contains something to start — and launch the bootloader on it.

There are two kinds of firmware:

**UEFI** (Unified Extensible Firmware Interface) is the modern standard, on essentially every computer made in the last decade. UEFI understands files and folders. It looks on the disk for a special area called the **EFI System Partition** (ESP) — a small storage area formatted in the simple FAT32 format — and runs a bootloader file stored there, such as `grubx64.efi`.

**BIOS** (Basic Input/Output System) is the old way, from before UEFI. Instead of reading a file, the BIOS read the raw first chunk of the disk (the very first 512 bytes) and ran whatever code was there. It works, but it's clumsy and limited. You'll meet it mostly on older machines.

The key difference: UEFI reads a real *file* from a known partition; old BIOS read raw *bytes* from the start of the disk. Either way, the result is the same — the bootloader now runs.

**Recap:** firmware is built into the motherboard, checks the hardware, finds a boot disk, and launches the bootloader. UEFI (modern) does this by reading a file from the EFI System Partition; BIOS (old) did it by reading the first raw sector.

items.prompt: What does the word "firmware" mean in this context?
choices: A program you download and install after buying the computer
Software permanently stored in a chip on the motherboard, so it is always available
A type of hard disk
The login screen of the operating system
items.explanation: Firmware is software "baked into" hardware — it lives in a motherboard chip and is there the instant power arrives. That is exactly why it can run before any disk is read.

items.prompt: What are the two main jobs of the firmware when you power on?
choices: Show the desktop and load your documents
Check the hardware (POST), then find a boot device and launch its bootloader
Encrypt the disk and connect to Wi-Fi
Start systemd and reach the login prompt
items.explanation: Firmware does a Power-On Self-Test to verify the hardware, then locates a disk to boot from and hands off to its bootloader. Desktops, logins, and systemd all come much later in the chain.

items.prompt: What does POST (Power-On Self-Test) do?
choices: It posts a message to a remote server
It quickly checks that basic hardware like memory and disks are present and working
It loads the kernel into memory
It logs the user in automatically
items.explanation: POST is the firmware's quick hardware self-check at power-on. It is unrelated to networking, and loading the kernel or logging in happens in later stages.

items.prompt: Which firmware type is the modern standard found on essentially all recent computers?
choices: BIOS
UEFI
GRUB
POST
items.explanation: UEFI is today's standard. BIOS is the older approach it replaced, GRUB is a bootloader (a later stage), and POST is the hardware self-test, not a firmware type.

items.prompt: What is the EFI System Partition (ESP)?
choices: The partition that holds all your personal documents
A small FAT32-formatted area on the disk where UEFI finds and runs bootloader files
A chip on the motherboard that stores the firmware
The part of memory the kernel runs in
items.explanation: The ESP is a small, simply-formatted (FAT32) partition that UEFI reads to find a bootloader file like grubx64.efi. It is on the disk, not a motherboard chip, and it does not store your documents.

items.prompt: What is the key difference in HOW UEFI versus old BIOS launches the bootloader?
choices: UEFI reads a real file from the EFI System Partition; BIOS read raw bytes from the disk's first 512-byte sector
UEFI uses the keyboard; BIOS uses the mouse
UEFI runs after the kernel; BIOS runs before the firmware
There is no difference; the names are interchangeable
items.explanation: UEFI understands files and reads a bootloader file from the ESP. Old BIOS instead executed the raw code in the disk's very first sector. The end result is the same — the bootloader starts — but the method differs.

items.prompt: Why does the firmware need to run before the bootloader, kernel, or init?
choices: Because the bootloader refuses to start without the firmware's permission code
Because at power-on nothing useful is in memory yet, and only the firmware (in a motherboard chip) is already there to run
Because the firmware is faster than the other stages
Because the kernel is too large to load first
items.explanation: Right at power-on the disk has not been read, so memory holds nothing useful. The firmware is the one program physically present in a chip, so it must run first and find the disk for everything else.

items.prompt: What format is the EFI System Partition typically stored in, and why does that matter?
choices: FAT32, a simple format the firmware can read without needing complex drivers
An encrypted format, so no one can read the bootloader
The same format as the rest of Linux, so the firmware needs no special handling
A raw, unformatted area with no filesystem at all
items.explanation: The ESP uses FAT32, a deliberately simple and universal format that UEFI firmware can read easily. A more complex or encrypted filesystem would be harder for the lightweight firmware to handle this early.

items.prompt: After the firmware finishes its work, what happens next in the boot chain?
choices: The login prompt appears immediately
The bootloader it found and launched begins running
The computer powers off
The kernel starts init directly, skipping the bootloader
items.explanation: The firmware's last act is to launch the bootloader, so the bootloader runs next. The kernel, init, and login all come afterward, in that order.

items.prompt: In the diagram, the UEFI firmware looks at the disk and reads one partition first before anything else. Which one?
choices: The main partition holding the root filesystem
The EFI System Partition (ESP), which holds the bootloader file
A hidden recovery partition
It reads the whole disk at once
items.explanation: UEFI reads the small FAT32 EFI System Partition first to find the bootloader (for example grubx64.efi). The larger main/root partition is used later, after the kernel has started.

lessons.title: The Bootloader: GRUB Loads the Kernel
lessons.content:
The firmware found a disk and launched a program on it. That program is the **bootloader**, and on most Linux desktops and servers it is **GRUB** (GRand Unified Bootloader). The bootloader is the bridge between firmware and the operating system.

Why do we even need a separate bootloader? The firmware is simple and limited — it just knows how to find and start one file. The bootloader is smarter: it knows where the operating system's **kernel** is stored, how to load it into memory, and how to start it. Think of GRUB as a receptionist who greets you at the door and walks you to the right room.

GRUB does three things:

1. **Shows a menu** (sometimes only briefly). If a computer has more than one operating system, or several kernel versions, GRUB lets you pick. Often it just auto-selects the default after a moment.
2. **Loads the kernel** — it reads the kernel file from the disk into memory.
3. **Passes along instructions** — GRUB hands the kernel a short line of text called the **kernel command line**: settings the kernel reads as it starts, such as which disk holds the main system. (More on that next lesson.)

You usually don't write GRUB's menu by hand. It's generated from configuration files, and a command regenerates the menu when you change something — but as a beginner you mostly just need to recognize GRUB's menu when you see it at boot.

A quick contrast: small embedded gadgets (think a router or a single-board computer with an ARM chip) often use a different bootloader called **U-Boot** instead of GRUB. Same idea — load the kernel — just a different tool for tiny hardware.

**Recap:** the bootloader (GRUB on most PCs) loads the kernel into memory, optionally lets you choose which one, and passes it a kernel command line. It exists because the firmware is too simple to do this itself.

items.prompt: What is the bootloader's central job?
choices: To test the hardware at power-on
To load the operating system's kernel into memory and start it
To display the desktop wallpaper
To format the EFI System Partition
items.explanation: The bootloader loads and launches the kernel. Hardware testing is the firmware's job, and desktops appear far later, after init has done its work.

items.prompt: What does GRUB stand for, and what kind of program is it?
choices: A firmware chip on the motherboard
The GRand Unified Bootloader, the common bootloader on Linux PCs and servers
A part of the Linux kernel
A type of disk partition
items.explanation: GRUB is the GRand Unified Bootloader — the bootloader stage that runs after the firmware. It is not firmware, not part of the kernel, and not a partition.

items.prompt: Why is a separate bootloader needed instead of letting the firmware load the kernel directly?
choices: The firmware is forbidden by law from loading a kernel
The firmware is simple and only knows how to start one file; the bootloader is smarter about finding and loading the kernel
The kernel can only be loaded by another kernel
The bootloader is faster than the firmware at testing memory
items.explanation: Firmware is intentionally minimal — it just launches one file. The bootloader adds the smarts to locate the kernel, load it, and pass it settings. It is a division of labor, not a legal rule.

items.prompt: What is the "kernel command line" that GRUB passes to the kernel?
choices: A line of text with startup settings the kernel reads, such as which disk holds the main system
The command you type to log in
A list of all the files on the disk
The serial number of the motherboard
items.explanation: The kernel command line is a short string of startup options the kernel reads as it boots. It is set by GRUB, not typed by the user at login, and it is not a file list or a serial number.

items.prompt: Why does GRUB sometimes show a menu during boot?
choices: To let you type your password
To let you choose between multiple operating systems or kernel versions if more than one is installed
To run the Power-On Self-Test
To format the disk before booting
items.explanation: The GRUB menu exists so you can pick which OS or kernel to start when several are available; otherwise it usually auto-selects a default. Passwords, POST, and formatting are not what the menu is for.

items.prompt: Which bootloader is commonly used on small embedded devices with ARM chips, instead of GRUB?
choices: systemd
U-Boot
POST
FAT32
items.explanation: U-Boot is the bootloader often used on tiny ARM-based hardware like routers and single-board computers. systemd is init (a much later stage), POST is a hardware test, and FAT32 is a disk format.

items.prompt: Where does the bootloader sit in the boot chain?
choices: Before the firmware
Between the firmware and the kernel
After init (systemd)
At the very end, right before login
items.explanation: The firmware launches the bootloader, and the bootloader loads the kernel — so it sits squarely between the two. It does not run before the firmware or after init.

items.prompt: As a beginner, what is the most important thing to recognize about GRUB's menu?
choices: You must hand-write the whole menu file before every boot
It is the screen where you can pick which operating system or kernel to start
It is the same as the login screen
It only appears after the kernel has fully loaded
items.explanation: Recognizing GRUB's menu — the boot-time screen for choosing an OS or kernel — is the practical takeaway. The menu is generated from config (not hand-written each boot), it is not the login screen, and it appears before the kernel loads, not after.

items.prompt: Using the receptionist analogy, GRUB "walks you to the right room." What does the "right room" represent?
choices: The login prompt
The correct kernel to load and start
The motherboard firmware
The Wi-Fi network
items.explanation: The analogy means GRUB directs the boot to the correct kernel and loads it. The login prompt comes much later, the firmware ran earlier, and networking is unrelated to GRUB's core job.

lessons.title: The Kernel Starts — and Why initramfs Exists
lessons.content:
GRUB has loaded the **kernel** — the core of Linux — into memory and started it. The kernel now takes charge of the hardware: it sets up memory, detects devices, and prepares to run programs. Its big goal at this point is to mount the **root filesystem**: the main disk where all the system's files live, the one that becomes "`/`".

But there's a chicken-and-egg problem. To read a disk, the kernel needs the right **driver** — a small piece of code that knows how to talk to that specific kind of hardware. Modern disks come in many flavors: fast NVMe drives, encrypted volumes, RAID setups. The kernel can't include every possible driver built-in (it would be huge), so the driver it needs to reach the real disk might not be loaded yet. The kernel can't mount a disk it can't talk to.

The clever solution is the **initramfs** (initial RAM filesystem). Along with the kernel, GRUB also loaded a second small file: a tiny, temporary filesystem that lives entirely in memory. The initramfs contains just enough drivers and tools to find and open the real disk.

Think of it like a spare key kept under the doormat. You can't get into the house without the key, and the key isn't *inside* the house — so you keep a small one outside, just enough to open the front door. Once you're in, you don't need it anymore.

The sequence: the kernel mounts the initramfs first, runs the drivers inside it, uses them to find and mount the real root disk, then **switches over** to that real disk as "`/`". The initramfs is then thrown away, freeing the memory.

**Recap:** the kernel needs to mount the real disk, but might lack the driver to reach it. The initramfs is a tiny temporary in-memory filesystem carrying those drivers — a stepping-stone that gets the kernel to the real root, then disappears.

items.prompt: What is the kernel's main goal once GRUB has started it?
choices: To show the GRUB menu
To take charge of the hardware and mount the real root filesystem (the main disk, "/")
To run the Power-On Self-Test
To pick which operating system to boot
items.explanation: After starting, the kernel controls the hardware and works toward mounting the root filesystem so the system's files become available. The GRUB menu, POST, and OS selection all happened in earlier stages.

items.prompt: What is the initramfs FOR? (the key idea of this lesson)
choices: It is a permanent backup copy of all your files
It is a tiny temporary in-memory filesystem holding the drivers the kernel needs to find and mount the real disk
It is the firmware stored on the motherboard
It is the login screen shown before the desktop
items.explanation: The initramfs is a small, temporary RAM-based filesystem that carries just enough drivers and tools to reach the real root disk. It is not a backup, not firmware, and not the login screen.

items.prompt: What is a "driver" in this context?
choices: A person who maintains the computer
A small piece of code that knows how to talk to a specific kind of hardware, like a particular disk
Another name for the kernel command line
The cable connecting the disk to the motherboard
items.explanation: A driver is software that lets the kernel communicate with a specific device. Without the right driver, the kernel cannot read that disk — which is exactly the problem the initramfs solves.

items.prompt: Why might the kernel be unable to mount the real disk on its own, right away?
choices: Because the disk is always encrypted by law
Because the kernel can't build in every possible driver, so the driver for that exact disk type may not be loaded yet
Because GRUB deletes the disk during boot
Because the firmware locks the disk until login
items.explanation: Including every driver would make the kernel enormous, so the needed driver for a particular NVMe, encrypted, or RAID disk may not be present initially. The kernel cannot mount hardware it cannot yet talk to.

items.prompt: In the "spare key under the doormat" analogy, what does the spare key represent?
choices: Your login password
The initramfs — just enough to open the door (mount the real disk), kept outside the house
The firmware in the motherboard chip
The GRUB menu
items.explanation: The spare key is the initramfs: a small thing kept outside the "house" (the real disk) that holds just enough to open the front door. Once inside, it is no longer needed — just like the initramfs is discarded after the real root is mounted.

items.prompt: Which describes the correct sequence the kernel follows with the initramfs?
choices: Mount the real disk, then load the initramfs, then delete the kernel
Mount the initramfs first, use its drivers to find and mount the real root disk, then switch to the real disk and discard the initramfs
Run the initramfs as the permanent root forever
Skip the initramfs and ask the firmware to mount the disk
items.explanation: The kernel mounts the temporary initramfs, runs its drivers to reach the real root, switches over to that real disk, and then frees the initramfs. It is a stepping-stone, never the permanent root.

items.prompt: What happens to the initramfs after the real root filesystem is mounted?
choices: It stays in memory permanently as the main filesystem
It is thrown away, freeing the memory it used
It is copied onto the disk for next time
It becomes the kernel
items.explanation: Once the real root is mounted, the initramfs has done its job and is discarded so its memory can be reused. It is temporary by design and does not become the kernel or get saved to disk.

items.prompt: Where does the initramfs live while it is in use?
choices: On the EFI System Partition
Entirely in memory (RAM)
Inside the motherboard firmware chip
On the real root disk it is trying to mount
items.explanation: The initramfs is an "initial RAM filesystem" — it lives in memory. It cannot live on the real root disk, since its whole purpose is to reach that disk in the first place.

items.prompt: What does it mean that the kernel "mounts" the root filesystem?
choices: It physically screws the disk into the case
It makes the disk's files available to the system, attached at the path "/"
It deletes everything on the disk
It copies the disk into the firmware
items.explanation: Mounting means making a filesystem's contents accessible, attached at a location in the directory tree — here, at the root "/". It has nothing to do with physically installing the drive or erasing it.

items.prompt: Two files are loaded together by GRUB at boot. What are they?
choices: The firmware and the BIOS
The kernel and the initramfs
The login screen and the desktop
The EFI System Partition and the root disk
items.explanation: GRUB loads both the kernel and its initramfs into memory. The firmware/BIOS run before GRUB, and the login screen and desktop come much later via init.

items.prompt: The diagram shows three steps. Which step lets the kernel finally reach the real disk?
choices: Step 1, where the kernel starts but cannot yet read the real disk
Step 2, where the initramfs supplies the drivers needed to reach the disk
There is no step that reaches the disk
Step 1, because the kernel never needs any drivers
items.explanation: In step 1 the kernel lacks the driver; step 2 is the initramfs providing that driver so the kernel can mount the real root in step 3. The initramfs is the bridge between not-reachable and reachable.

lessons.title: The Handoff to init and systemd
lessons.content:
The kernel has mounted the real disk. Now comes a special moment: the kernel starts the very first ordinary program. That program becomes **PID 1** — process number 1, the first process — and it is called **init**. On modern Linux, init is a program named **systemd**.

This is the boundary between two worlds. Before this moment, the kernel was running everything itself ("kernel space"). After it, normal programs run on top of the kernel ("user space"). The kernel doesn't stop — it keeps running underneath, managing hardware — but from now on it starts programs *as requested* rather than driving the boot itself.

PID 1 is special in two ways. It is the **ancestor of every other program**: everything else is started by it, or by something it started. And it **never exits** while the computer is on — if PID 1 stopped, the system would have no one in charge.

What does systemd actually do? It starts all the services that make a usable system: networking, logging, the sound system, the graphical desktop, and finally the **login prompt**. It starts many of them **in parallel** (at the same time) where it safely can, which is part of why modern systems boot quickly.

A bit of history: before systemd, the classic init was **SysV init**, which started services strictly one after another using numbered scripts and "runlevels" (numbered system states). systemd replaced runlevels with **targets** — named goals like `multi-user.target` (a full text-mode system) or `graphical.target` (a full desktop). You don't need to memorize these as a beginner; just know that a "target" is systemd's word for "the state we're booting toward."

**Recap:** the kernel starts init (systemd) as PID 1 — the first process, ancestor of all others, which never exits. systemd then starts the system's services, often in parallel, ending at the login prompt. This is the final handoff in the boot chain.

items.prompt: What is PID 1?
choices: The first hard disk in the computer
Process number 1 — the very first ordinary program the kernel starts, called init
The first partition on the disk
The firmware's identification number
items.explanation: PID 1 is the first process: the init program the kernel launches once the real disk is mounted. It is not a disk, partition, or firmware ID.

items.prompt: On a modern Linux system, which program is init (PID 1)?
choices: GRUB
systemd
The kernel
UEFI
items.explanation: Today systemd is init. GRUB is the bootloader, the kernel is a separate (earlier) thing that starts init, and UEFI is firmware from the very start of the chain.

items.prompt: Why is the start of PID 1 described as a "boundary"?
choices: It is where the disk is physically partitioned
It marks the switch from the kernel driving the boot (kernel space) to normal programs running on top of it (user space)
It is the boundary between two different disks
It is where the firmware hands off to GRUB
items.explanation: Starting PID 1 is the crossing point from kernel space (the kernel running the show) to user space (ordinary programs). The firmware-to-GRUB handoff happened much earlier in the chain.

items.prompt: What makes PID 1 special compared to ordinary programs?
choices: It runs only for one second and then quits
It is the ancestor of every other program and never exits while the computer is on
It runs inside the firmware chip
It is the only program that can read the disk
items.explanation: PID 1 starts everything else (directly or indirectly) and must keep running the whole time the system is up. If it exited, nothing would be in charge. It runs in user space, not in the firmware.

items.prompt: Does the kernel stop running once init (systemd) starts?
choices: Yes, the kernel shuts down and systemd takes its place entirely
No, the kernel keeps running underneath, managing hardware, while programs run on top of it
Yes, but only until login
No, but the kernel is copied into the initramfs
items.explanation: The kernel never stops while the system runs; it continues managing hardware beneath user-space programs. init does not replace the kernel — it runs on top of it.

items.prompt: What does systemd do after it becomes PID 1?
choices: It tests the memory and finds a boot disk
It starts the system's services (networking, logging, desktop) and finally the login prompt
It loads the kernel into memory
It runs the GRUB menu
items.explanation: systemd's job as init is to bring up all the services that make a usable system, ending at the login prompt. Memory testing/boot-disk finding is the firmware's job, and loading the kernel is GRUB's.

items.prompt: One reason modern systems boot quickly is that systemd does what?
choices: Skips starting most services
Starts many services in parallel (at the same time) where it safely can
Runs entirely inside the firmware
Boots without a kernel
items.explanation: systemd starts independent services in parallel rather than strictly one-by-one, which speeds up boot. It does not skip needed services, and it certainly still requires the kernel.

items.prompt: How did the older SysV init differ from systemd?
choices: SysV started services strictly one after another using numbered scripts and runlevels
SysV ran before the firmware
SysV was a type of disk
SysV did not need a kernel
items.explanation: The classic SysV init started services serially (one at a time) via numbered scripts and runlevels. systemd replaced that with parallel startup and named targets. Both run as init, after the kernel.

items.prompt: In systemd, what is a "target" (such as graphical.target)?
choices: A named goal describing the state the system is booting toward, like a full desktop
A physical disk to boot from
A driver inside the initramfs
A setting in the motherboard firmware
items.explanation: A target is systemd's word for a state to reach, such as graphical.target (full desktop) or multi-user.target (full text-mode). It replaced the older numbered runlevels and has nothing to do with disks or firmware.

items.prompt: Putting it together: which is the correct final stretch of the boot chain?
choices: Kernel mounts the real disk → starts init (systemd) as PID 1 → systemd starts services → login prompt
systemd starts the kernel → kernel finds the disk → login → PID 1
Login prompt → kernel → systemd → disk mounted
Kernel → firmware → systemd → GRUB → login
items.explanation: After mounting the real root, the kernel starts systemd as PID 1, which brings up services and ends at the login prompt. The kernel starts systemd — not the other way around — and firmware/GRUB ran long before this stage.

items.prompt: The diagram divides the picture into "kernel space" and "user space." What is the single arrow between them?
choices: The firmware testing the hardware
The handoff where the kernel starts init (PID 1, systemd) in user space
GRUB loading the kernel
The login prompt appearing
items.explanation: The arrow is the handoff: the kernel (kernel space) starts init as PID 1 in user space. GRUB loading the kernel and the firmware's hardware test were earlier stages, and login comes after this handoff.

lessons.title: When Boot Goes Wrong: Diagnostics
lessons.content:
Now the payoff. You understand the chain: firmware → bootloader → kernel → initramfs → init → login. When a machine misbehaves — boots slowly, or a service fails — Linux keeps detailed records of what happened, and a few commands let you read them. You don't need to memorize every option; just know which tool answers which question.

**`dmesg`** shows the **kernel's messages** — the running log the kernel keeps from the moment it started: which devices it found, which drivers it loaded, any hardware complaints. If a disk or network card isn't working, `dmesg` often shows the kernel struggling with it. ("dmesg" stands for "display message," referring to the kernel's message buffer.)

**`journalctl -b`** shows the **logs from the current boot**. systemd keeps a unified journal of everything that happened since power-on — every service starting, succeeding, or failing. The `-b` part means "this boot only." This is where you look when a *service* (not the hardware) failed to start. To see kernel-only messages from this boot, you can add `-k`.

**`systemd-analyze`** answers "**why was boot slow?**" On its own it prints how long each phase took (firmware, loader, kernel, userspace). Adding `blame` lists the slowest services, so you can see what dragged boot down.

The mental model: a hardware/driver problem? Reach for `dmesg`. A service that won't start? Reach for `journalctl -b`. A slow boot? Reach for `systemd-analyze`. These three cover most boot troubleshooting, and they all build on the same chain you've learned — each tool reports on a different stage of it.

**Recap:** `dmesg` for kernel/hardware messages, `journalctl -b` for this-boot service logs (`-k` for kernel-only), and `systemd-analyze` (with `blame`) for boot timing. Match the tool to the question, and the boot chain becomes something you can debug, not just describe.

items.prompt: Which command shows the kernel's own messages, such as which devices and drivers it found?
choices: dmesg
systemd-analyze blame
ls /boot
efibootmgr
items.explanation: dmesg displays the kernel's message buffer — device detection, driver loading, and hardware warnings. systemd-analyze is about boot timing, and the others are unrelated to reading kernel messages.

items.prompt: You suspect a hardware or driver problem during boot — a disk or network card that won't come up. Which tool should you reach for first?
choices: systemd-analyze
dmesg
The GRUB menu
POST
items.explanation: Hardware and driver issues show up in the kernel's messages, which dmesg displays. systemd-analyze is for timing, the GRUB menu is for choosing a kernel, and POST is the firmware's early self-test.

items.prompt: What does `journalctl -b` show?
choices: Only the firmware's settings
The logs from the current boot — systemd's unified record of what happened since power-on
A list of files in /boot
The contents of the EFI System Partition
items.explanation: journalctl reads systemd's journal, and -b limits it to the current boot: every service starting, succeeding, or failing since power-on. It is not a firmware viewer or a file lister.

items.prompt: A particular service failed to start during boot. Which command is best for investigating that?
choices: dmesg
journalctl -b
systemd-analyze
efibootmgr
items.explanation: Service start/fail records live in systemd's journal, so journalctl -b is the right tool. dmesg focuses on the kernel/hardware, and systemd-analyze is about how long boot took, not why a service failed.

items.prompt: Your computer boots, but slowly. Which command answers "why was boot slow?"
choices: dmesg
journalctl -k
systemd-analyze (especially with 'blame')
ls /
items.explanation: systemd-analyze reports how long each boot phase took, and systemd-analyze blame lists the slowest services. dmesg and journalctl -k show kernel messages, which are about correctness, not timing.

items.prompt: What does the `-b` in `journalctl -b` mean?
choices: Show messages in the background
Limit the output to the current boot only
Show only the bootloader's messages
Sort the output alphabetically
items.explanation: -b narrows the journal to the current boot, so you see only what happened since the last power-on. It is not about backgrounding, the bootloader specifically, or sorting.

items.prompt: How would you see kernel-only messages from the current boot using journalctl?
choices: journalctl -b -k
journalctl --firmware
journalctl --grub
journalctl -b --slow
items.explanation: Adding -k to journalctl -b filters to kernel messages from this boot — similar to dmesg but as a journal query. The other options are not real options.

items.prompt: Match the tool to the question: "Which services took the longest to start?"
choices: dmesg
systemd-analyze blame
journalctl -k
efibootmgr
items.explanation: systemd-analyze blame lists services ordered by how long they took to start, directly answering this question. dmesg and journalctl -k are about kernel messages, not service timing.

items.prompt: Why is knowing the boot chain helpful for using these diagnostic tools?
choices: It isn't — the tools work without any understanding
Each tool reports on a different stage of the chain, so knowing the stages tells you which tool to use
The tools only run if you can recite the chain out loud
The chain replaces the need for any diagnostic tools
items.explanation: Because each tool covers a different part of the chain (kernel/hardware, services, timing), knowing the chain points you to the right tool for a given symptom. Understanding does not replace the tools, but it makes them far more useful.

items.prompt: Pick the correct tool-to-question matching for all three tools.
choices: dmesg = slow boot; journalctl -b = hardware; systemd-analyze = service failed
dmesg = kernel/hardware messages; journalctl -b = this-boot service logs; systemd-analyze = boot timing
dmesg = login problems; journalctl -b = firmware settings; systemd-analyze = disk formatting
All three do exactly the same thing
items.explanation: dmesg reports kernel/hardware messages, journalctl -b shows this boot's service logs, and systemd-analyze reports boot timing. Matching the symptom to the right tool is the practical skill of this lesson.
